title: The Achaemenid Empire
description: How well do you know the Achaemenid Empire? Let's do a quick little check-up before we move on.

assessment_items.question_content:
Cyrus the Great’s policy for incorporating new regions into his empire was
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Cyrus reasoned that a stable province would provide more tribute than an unstable one.

The example of Cyrus's policy in allowing the Jews to return to Israel and establish a tributary state is instructive, here—he was invested in ensuring loyalty from a conquered population by returning some cultural sovereignty to them.

choices.content: Allow local traditions and rulers to remain in place
Remove any trace of pre-Persian rule
Destroy and rebuild the cities he conquered

hints: A client state doesn't control other states.
A client state, unlike a colony, has some measure of independence.

choices.content: is indirectly under the control of an imperial state
is directly governed by an imperial state

hints: Darius reorganized the empire by appointing regional governors.
The concentrated wealth enabled by a standard currency allowed Darius to build a new imperial capital in a well-defended, remote location.
Darius had an interest in making sure that trade and communication could take place easily within his empire.

choices.content: built a new capital at Persepolis
instituted the use of satrapies to govern the empire

assessment_items.question_content:
The major cause of the fall of the Achaemenid empire was

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Food shortages did not contribute to the decline of the Achaemenid empire.
Greek city-states in Ionia rebelled against Achaemenid rule in 499 BCE.
Alexander of Macedon's invasion of the Persian Empire was profoundly destabilizing to Persia.
